step.title: Step 55
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 18 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 128, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Pressing SPACE below the left frame created a 'G' frame at row 32-35, col 16-19. The white head shrank to row 36. The left frame at row 28-31 is still 'c'.
- Goal model: Need to collect the 'G' frames. Pressing SPACE again might collect it.
- Plan: Try pressing SPACE again to see if it collects the 'G' frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 127 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, I can see the player has moved to the left. There's a green body visible on the left edge. Let me check the current state to see what happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Current frames:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G', 'b'] and n['pixels'] > 10:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nPlayer:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is now at row 16-19, col 0-3. The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. The player is way above and to the left of the left frame.
I moved too far LEFT and UP. The player went off the left edge of the board and wrapped around or something.
Let me reconsider the approach. I need to be more careful about positioning.
The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. To collect it, I need to:
1. Position player directly below the frame (row 32, col 16-19)
2. Press UP to make the frame 'G'
3. Press SPACE to collect it
Currently, the player is at row 16-19, col 0-3. I need to move RIGHT and DOWN to get below the left frame.
To get to row 32, col 16-19:
- Move RIGHT by 16 columns (from col 0 to col 16)
- Move DOWN by 16 rows (from row 16 to row 32)
But wait, the player's white head is at row 16, col 0-3. The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. So I need to move RIGHT by 16 columns and DOWN by 12 rows (from row 16 to row 28, then the player would be at the top of the frame).
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The player's white head is at row 16, col 0-3. The left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. To be directly below the left frame, I need the player's white head to be at row 32, col 16-19.
So I need to move RIGHT by 16 columns and DOWN by 16 rows.
But wait, I also need to consider that the player might be blocked by the center frame (b) at row 28-31, col 28-39. Let me check if the path is clear.
The left frame is at col 16-19, and the center frame is at col 28-39. So there's a gap between them. The player should be able to move RIGHT from col 0 to col 16 without hitting the center frame.
Let me try: RIGHT * 16 + DOWN * 16 + UP + SPACE.
Wait, that would put the player at row 32, col 16-19, then UP would move the player to row 28, col 16-19 (inside the left frame), and SPACE would collect it.
Actually, I realize I've been making this too complicated. Let me step back and think about the game mechanics more carefully.
Looking at the right frame collection:
- History[38]: Player was at row 28-31, col 44-47 (green body) and row 28, col 47 (white head). The right frame was at row 24-27, col 44-47.
- History[39]: Player pressed UP. The right frame became 'G'. Player white head at row 28, col 44-47.
- History[40]: Player pressed SPACE. The right frame disappeared.
So the sequence was:
1. Player positioned below the frame (white head at row 28, which is below the frame at row 24-27)
2. Press UP - frame becomes 'G'
3. Press SPACE - frame disappears
For the left frame at row 28-31, col 16-19:
- To be directly below, player needs white head at row 32, col 16-19.
- Currently player is at row 16, col 0-3.
- Need to move RIGHT 16 and DOWN 16.
Let me try this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is at row 16-19, col 0-3
# Left frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19
# Need to position player directly below left frame: white head at row 32, col 16-19
# Move RIGHT 16 and DOWN 16

action(['RIGHT'] * 16 + ['DOWN'] * 16 + ['UP', 'SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
